nodes.label: activity_events Hypertable
nodes.rationale: Highest-volume table, partitioned on started_at with 7-day chunks; PK is composite (user_id, started_at, event_id) because TimescaleDB requires the partitioning column in every unique constraint; the UNIQUE (user_id, event_id, started_at) constraint is the idempotency key; chunks older than 30 days compress; retention is configurable, not fixed.

nodes.label: sync_changelog Outbox Table
nodes.rationale: One append-only heap row per write to any of six syncable tables via AFTER INSERT/UPDATE triggers, committed in the same transaction as the entity write so commit ordering is authoritative for the pull cursor — a plain heap stays readable even after hypertable chunks compress; backfill seeded one row per pre-existing entity row.

nodes.label: sync_changelog_horizon Singleton Watermark
nodes.rationale: Single permanent row (bool PK DEFAULT true CHECK (id) singleton pattern) tracking the furthest (xid8, server_seq) position ever pruned; advanced with GREATEST in the same transaction as each retention batch delete, never regressed. The 90-day retention default bounds the xid8 wraparound-safety window from migrations 000024/000025.

nodes.label: Continuous Aggregates (daily_app_totals, daily_category_totals, hourly_category_totals)
nodes.rationale: Chosen over hand-rolled rollups for automatic refresh, correctness under late/out-of-order offline-first inserts, and compression-awareness; all three are device-aware (device_id in GROUP BY, migrations 000030-000037) and real-time (materialized_only = false) so just-closed periods are immediately correct.

nodes.label: refresh_tokens Table (rotation families)
nodes.rationale: family_id groups rotations for reuse detection; token_hash index makes per-request validation a single indexed lookup; family_id index makes whole-family revocation cheap.

nodes.label: users Table (identity + lockout state)
nodes.rationale: citext email gives case-insensitive uniqueness without app-side normalization; failed_login_attempts/lockout_count/locked_until (migration 000028) back the escalating lockout policy; users has server_seq but is intentionally not in the pull feed — profile data flows through auth endpoints instead.

nodes.label: devices Table
nodes.rationale: Globally unique id scoped to one user_id subsumes the UNIQUE (user_id, device_id) semantics the sync protocol requires, so cross-user device-id collisions are impossible at registration; same-user reuse (backup restore) is tolerated, not detected.

nodes.label: focus_sessions Table
nodes.rationale: ended_at is nullable and status can be 'running' because a session row is created at start, not finish; unresolved sessions are reconciled by the sync protocol.

nodes.label: deletion_requests Table (GDPR grace period)
nodes.rationale: executed_at remaining NULL distinguishes a pending, cancellable request from an executed one, implementing the 7-day GDPR grace period.

nodes.label: Client-supplied UUIDv7 Primary Keys
nodes.rationale: Client-originated rows use time-ordered UUIDv7 generated before the first network attempt, so the identifier itself is the idempotency key — retries can never create duplicates and no server round-trip is needed to allocate an id.

nodes.label: server_seq Per-row Change Counter
nodes.rationale: Bumped on every write to a syncable row; remains the per-row counter returned to clients, but the pull cursor itself is an opaque commit-ordered watermark over sync_changelog, not a WHERE server_seq > :cursor scan of each table.

nodes.label: Soft Delete via deleted_at / deleted Flag
nodes.rationale: Soft delete lets sync propagate tombstones to other devices and keeps deletions reversible until the GDPR grace period expires; activity_events uses a plain boolean flag instead of deleted_at, consistent with append-only rows never being truly mutated.
